meta.title: Start Here
meta.subBanner: Module 0 • Course Setup • Get Ready for Python

sections.html: Welcome to IS2053! Before diving into Python, let’s make sure you’re fully set up for success. Complete each checkpoint below in order—they’re designed to build on each other. Most students finish in 80–110 minutes.

sections.name: Danny Cavanaugh
sections.quote: “Hey there! I’m Danny, and I’ll be your guide through this course. Don’t rush through these setup steps—a solid foundation saves hours of frustration later. If you hit any snags, swing by our Tuesday Zoom session. We’re here to help!”

preContent.html: Twelve checkpoints. Plan on 80–110 minutes total. Each checkpoint shows its time estimate so you can split this across multiple sittings if you need to.

preContent.title: Don’t Rush the Installs
preContent.body_html: Checkpoints 3–9 set up your entire development environment. If something goes wrong here, every future assignment gets harder. Take your time and verify each step works before moving on.

needHelp.title: Need Help?
needHelp.subBanner: Look No Further! We’ve Got You

items: Re-read the checkpoint you’re stuck on
Try a web search for Python installation issues
Post in the discussion forum (describe your issue clearly)
Come to Zoom office hours on Tuesday evenings

cards.label: Discord Community
cards.value_html: Get help from classmates and the instructor in #help-python. Fastest response time.

cards.label: Book a 1-on-1
cards.value_html:
15-minute focused session via Calendly
Book on Calendly

cards.label: Tuesday Office Hours
cards.value_html:
Tuesdays at 6:00 PM CT — drop-in for general questions. Not 1-on-1.
utsa.zoom.us/j/96542097913

body.html: The syllabus contains essential information about grading, policies, and course structure. Find it in the Syllabus link in the left navigation. Pay special attention to the grading breakdown and late work policy.

checkpoints.title: Read “The Scenario”
checkpoints.subBanner: 10 minutes — Meet the team and the client

body.html: Meet Bat City Collective, your mentors, the client (All My Eggses Live in Texas), and learn about the game you’ll be building all semester. This sets up the story and gives context to every lab assignment!
Download Python from python.org. Get version 3.14 or newer (3.14.5 is current as of Summer 2026).

body.title: Critical
body.body_html: During installation, check “Add Python to PATH”. Missing this step causes headaches later!

body.html: Download Visual Studio Code from code.visualstudio.com. This is our code editor for the semester—it’s free, powerful, and used by professional developers worldwide.

checkpoints.title: Disable AI Autocomplete in VS Code

body.html: VS Code now ships with AI-powered code suggestions turned on by default. It sounds helpful, but it will hurt you in this course. The AI doesn’t know what chapter we’re on, so it happily suggests list comprehensions, f-strings, and other techniques we haven’t covered yet. CodeGrade flags those as scope violations and auto-zeroes the assignment. The suggestions are also frequently just wrong for your specific problem.
Turn off all three of these in VS Code Settings (press Cmd + , on Mac or Ctrl + , on Windows):

items: Search editor.inlineSuggest.enabled — uncheck the box. This kills inline AI completions.
Search github.copilot.enable — set to false, or uninstall the GitHub Copilot extension entirely.
Search IntelliCode — disable or uninstall both the “IntelliCode” and “IntelliCode API Usage Examples” extensions.

body.title: How to Verify It Worked
body.body_html: After making these changes, when you type code you should only see basic autocomplete (Python keywords like if, while, print, and variable names you’ve already typed). You should not see entire lines of suggested code appearing in gray. Those gray ghost-lines are the AI—if you see them, you missed a setting.

body.html: I can’t enforce this from my end. But students who lean on AI suggestions in the early modules consistently struggle harder later, because they never built the foundation. See the full AI Policy for the longer version of why.

checkpoints.title: Install the Python Extension
checkpoints.subBanner: 2 minutes — Syntax highlighting and run button

body.html: In VS Code, click the Extensions icon (or press Ctrl+Shift+X). Search for “Python” and install the one by Microsoft. This gives you syntax highlighting, error detection, and the run button.
Still in Extensions, search for “Flake8” and install the one by Microsoft. Flake8 checks your code style—things like consistent spacing, proper indentation, and unused variables.

body.title: This Is Graded
body.body_html: CodeGrade uses Flake8 to check your code style on every submission. Installing it now means you’ll see the same warnings before you submit, so you can fix them and earn full points.

body.title: How It Works
body.body_html: After installing, you’ll see yellow/red squiggly lines under style issues in your code. Hover over them to see what’s wrong and how to fix it. We have a full Flake8 Guide in Canvas that explains every error code.

checkpoints.title: Set Up Your Workspace Folder
checkpoints.subBanner: 5 minutes — The folder structure that keeps you sane

body.html: You’re going to write a lot of code this semester. One folder per assignment is the simplest way to keep work organized, recover when something breaks, and avoid accidentally overwriting last week’s lab.
Download and unzip the starter folder:
Unzip it into your Documents folder (or anywhere easy to find that is not a cloud-synced folder — OneDrive and Dropbox cause file-locking issues you don’t want).
You’ll end up with this folder tree:

body.title: The One Rule That Makes Everything Easier
body.body_html:
Always “File → Open Folder” on the specific assignment folder you’re working in — never the parent is2053/ or code/ folder.

Why? When you open the assignment folder directly, Python automatically looks in that folder for data files like hazards.txt or city_info.txt. Open the parent folder instead, and Python looks in the parent — the data files are nowhere to be found and you get FileNotFoundError errors that make no sense.

Example for Lab 4.3: open is2053/code/modules/mod4/lab-4-3/ as the workspace, not is2053/. Drag the assignment folder onto the VS Code icon, or use File → Open Folder and pick it.

body.title: Why So Many Folders?
body.body_html: Modules 1–3 keep things simple — each lab is a single .py file in its own folder. Once you hit Module 4, your labs add a function_library.py module plus data files (city_distances.txt, hazards.txt, trivia.txt, etc.). Module 5 introduces multi-file class projects. Per-assignment folders isolate each lab’s data and prevent old files from leaking into new work.

checkpoints.title: Hello World — Your First Submission
checkpoints.subBanner: 15–20 minutes — Write a Python program, submit it to CodeGrade, get the painful first time out of the way

body.html: You’re going to do a complete end-to-end workflow for the first time: open a folder in VS Code, write a Python program, run it locally, then submit it to CodeGrade (the autograder we use for every coding assignment in this course). This submission is worth zero points — its only job is to make sure your setup works and to walk you through CodeGrade signup before there’s a grade on the line.

body.text: Step 1 — Open Folder, Not File (Your Day-One Workflow)

body.html: Right now you have two ways to get hello.py in front of VS Code. Pay attention to which one we’re using, because this is the workflow rule every assignment in this course depends on.

items: The wrong way — double-click hello.py in Finder/Explorer. VS Code opens with just that one file, disconnected from any folder. Python doesn’t know what directory to look in for data files. You won’t notice the problem today (there are no data files yet), but starting in Module 4 you’ll get FileNotFoundError messages that make no sense.

The right way — open the folder in VS Code first, then click the file from the sidebar. Python automatically uses that folder as its working directory. Your data files just work. This is how every professional Python developer works.

body.html: We’re going to do it the right way every single time, starting now. Even though hello.py has no data files and you could get away with the wrong way today, building the habit on day one means you never trip over the problem later.
To open the folder:

items: In Finder/Explorer, navigate to is2053/start_here/
Drag the start_here folder onto the VS Code icon — OR — open VS Code and use File → Open Folder, then pick start_here
You should now see HELLO.PY listed in the Explorer sidebar on the left. This means VS Code has the folder open as a workspace, and the file is accessible from inside it. That’s the workflow.

body.text: Step 2 — Write the program and run it locally

body.html: Now that the folder is open in VS Code, click hello.py in the Explorer sidebar on the left. It opens in the editor. Type exactly this line:
Save the file (Cmd + S on Mac, Ctrl + S on Windows). Then click the Play button in the upper-right corner of the editor. A terminal panel opens at the bottom of VS Code and you should see:
That’s your first Python program working locally. Now we send it to CodeGrade.

body.title: If the Play Button Doesn’t Work
body.body_html: The most common cause is Python wasn’t added to your PATH during install. If you see “Python is not recognized” or a similar error, head to Discord or grab a 1-on-1 via Calendly — we’ll get it sorted before you go further. Don’t skip ahead with a broken setup; everything from here on depends on it.

body.text: Step 3 — Submit to CodeGrade through Canvas

body.html: All assignments in this course are submitted through Canvas, but the actual grading runs in CodeGrade. The first time you submit, Canvas walks you through CodeGrade signup automatically — one-time setup, then every future submission goes straight through.

body._comment: Placeholder href '#' is replaced with the real Canvas URL once the assignment is set up. Stable id 'hello-world-canvas-link' is the rewrite target for Fall 2026 multi-section JS (see pages/support/json/sections.json).

items: Open Canvas and go to the Modules section
Find the “Hello World” assignment in Module 0 (Start Here)
Click the assignment, then click Load Hello World in a new window
A CodeGrade window opens. If this is your first time, it asks you to create an account using your UTSA email
CodeGrade asks for payment — see the callout below for your options
Once you’re paid up, you see the submission screen. Drag hello.py from your VS Code Explorer sidebar (or your Finder/Explorer) into the upload area
Click Submit

body.title: Payment Options
body.body_html:
CodeGrade access is a one-time fee for the semester. You have two choices:

  • Credit card — pay CodeGrade directly during signup. Fastest option, takes about 2 minutes.
  • Activation code from the bookstore — purchase one from the UT San Antonio Bookstore, then enter the code when CodeGrade prompts you. Use this if you’re paying with financial aid.

body.html: After submitting, CodeGrade processes your code and shows you a feedback panel. You should see:

items: A green checkmark or “passed” indicator on your submission
Your code displayed back to you in the right panel
A score of 0/0 (this assignment is zero points by design)

body.html: That’s the complete end-to-end workflow. From now on, every coding assignment in this course follows this exact pattern: open the assignment folder, write code, test locally, submit through Canvas. The signup pain is now behind you.
Need more detail on reading CodeGrade feedback later in the term? See the CodeGrade Guide for a complete walkthrough of correctness tests, scope compliance, and Flake8 style checks.

Our Discord server is where you can ask questions, get help from classmates, and share wins. Find the join link on the Discord page in the left navigation. Participation is optional but highly recommended!
Post in the Discord: Introductions forum. Tell us your name, major, and one thing you hope to build with Python someday.
You’re ready! Head to Module 1 and open Lab 1.1: Welcome to San Antonio. This is where you’ll write your first real Python program for the game.
Remember: Build a little, test it, fix it. Don’t try to write everything at once!
